g.data-narration: Welcome to Thread Scheduling - the essential mechanism that allows operating systems to manage multiple threads efficiently.
Modern systems have many more threads than CPU cores. A typical system might have hundreds of threads competing for just 4-8 cores.
g.text: Modern OS has hundreds of threads but only 4-8 CPU cores
How does the OS decide which thread runs and for how long?

g.data-narration: Thread Scheduling is how the OS kernel allocates precious CPU time slices to threads, ensuring fairness and optimal resource utilization.
g.text: The method by which the OS kernel allocates CPU time slices to threads
Goals: Maximize CPU utilization, ensure responsiveness, and maintain fairness

g.data-narration: The scheduler uses three key components: the ready queue holding waiting threads, context switching overhead, and the scheduling algorithm.
g.text: Threads in "Ready" state waiting for CPU
Save state of current thread, load new thread
Decides which thread gets CPU next

g.data-narration: FCFS is a simple non-preemptive algorithm where threads run in arrival order. It's easy to implement but can cause the convoy effect.
g.text: First-Come, First-Served: Threads run in arrival order until completion or block

g.data-narration: Round Robin assigns a fixed time quantum to each thread, cycling through the ready queue. It ensures fairness and good response time.
g.text: Each thread gets fixed time quantum (10-100ms), cycles through ready queue
Ensures fairness, good response time for interactive threads
⚠ Warning: Too small quantum = high context switch overhead

g.data-narration: Priority scheduling runs highest priority threads first. It can cause starvation, solved by aging - gradually increasing priority of waiting threads.
g.text: Highest priority thread gets CPU. Can be preemptive or non-preemptive
✓ Solution: Aging - priority increases with wait time

g.data-narration: User-Level Threads are managed by a thread library at the application level. The OS only sees the process, not individual threads.
g.text: Managed by thread library (e.g., POSIX Pthreads), OS only sees the process
⚠ If one ULT blocks (I/O), entire process blocks!

g.data-narration: Kernel-Level Threads are managed directly by the OS. The scheduler can map threads to CPU cores, enabling true parallel execution.
g.text: OS kernel manages threads directly, can schedule onto multiple CPU cores

g.data-narration: Modern systems use hybrid models - mapping many User-Level Threads to fewer Kernel-Level Threads, combining flexibility with efficiency.
g.text: Most OS (Windows, Linux) use hybrid: multiple ULTs mapped to smaller pool of KLTs

g.data-narration: Let's trace a scenario with Preemptive Priority Round-Robin: high priority thread runs, interactive thread yields early, and low priority thread gets CPU.
g.text: Three threads: A (High/CPU), B (Low/Interactive), C (Med/I/O) - Quantum = 30ms
4. Thread B returns, may preempt C

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: schedulers allocate CPU time, preemptive scheduling enables responsiveness, and algorithm choice impacts performance metrics.
g.text: • Ready Queue holds threads waiting for CPU
• Round Robin: Fair, uses time quantum
• Priority: Can cause starvation → use aging
• ULTs: Fast but whole process can block

g.data-narration: Thread scheduling is fundamental to OS design. Modern systems use hybrid models to balance flexibility and performance for seamless multi-tasking.
g.text: Understanding scheduling is crucial for designing efficient concurrent software
Modern OS (Windows/Linux) use hybrid models for optimal performance
